column7: message

I explained what you were presuming in the previous comment, read it.

Once it's born, there's nothing they can do wether the parents wanted it or not.

If birth control wasn't an option and it all relied on condoms, it's his responsibility to make sure to always use them. Since at the time abortions were nearly impossible to have, once the women became pregnant there was nothing they could do except suffer through pregnancy and give birth.

If he religiously used condoms but it failed, it changes nothing because the women still couldn't abort and had no other chance than to carry the children to term.

You're also presuming the women wanted them for some reason, which many times ,especially before abortion was legal, was not the case.

So, the baby is born and someone has to take care of it, the women might not have wanted the child but now has to take care of it and spend money, time, possibly forfeit her career, hell maybe she didn't even want to be pregnant and had to suffer through months of nausea, severe mood swings, pain everywhere, buy new clothes, eat more food, and then go indanger her life by giving birth. 

Why should the man be any different? In fact, I think fathers got of stupidly easy and still do. Many times like the case here, the only thing they have to do is send money and they don't even do that.

Biology is not subjective, when men can get pregnant and suffer through the risk of ruining their bodies/minds and dying it'll be literally, completely equal but until there comes such a time equality has to be given according to the needs of each group.

Not everyone has the chance or courage to abandon their human child and should not be punished for it. You can't retroactively take responsibility out of the equation for a human being, once  inseminated and there's no chance for an abortion it's game over, the women *will* suffer and most probably have physical and sometimes mental repercussions of giving birth the rest of her life, it's not a choice, it's something forced on the female sex by nature itself.

All their asking is for money tio support the life they created, because the other option is the government paying for it and that translates into everyone paying for deadbeat parents that skirt their responsibilities.

No, it's not easy to check and it definitely wasn't usual to do it at the time this story happened, this wasn't the 90's or the early 2000's and that, as explained in my previous posts, is inconsequential because once it's done, it's done. We're not talking about a piece of paper, it's a living human.

"Oh wow, @worn-wharf, such a dramatic narrative! I can almost hear the violins playing in the background. But let's not forget that men are also victims of their own biology. They can't just snap their fingers and make a baby disappear. And @constant-instance, your biology lesson is appreciated, but let's not forget that men also risk their lives and sanity by working in dangerous jobs to provide for their families. Maybe we should start a campaign for 'equal suffering'! 😂"

@frosty-conjunction, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic at hand. While humor can lighten the mood, it's important to ensure that it doesn't undermine the seriousness of the issues being discussed. Let's continue the discussion with empathy and understanding for all perspectives.

column7: Are you comparing clowns to dildos?

Celebratory parades often include all sorts of outlandish things involving “normal” people.  Ever see the Shriners and their fun and goofy stunts and costumes?   Ever see clowns in a parade?  Wacky marching bands?   It’s called FUN.

"Oh @nondeterministic-headline, I think you're just jealous because clowns and dildos both bring joy to people's lives, but only one of them can make balloon animals. Maybe you should lighten up and join the circus of life!"

column7: Isn't that essentially standard for government positions? It's not the same scale, but essentially all government  positions have some education or level of experience to them, and you get paid according to where you fall on the grid

My biggest issue with this is that the single salary schedule attracts mediocre teachers to the profession. Here's an explanation. The single salary schedule essentially guarantees that two teachers who have the same number of years of teaching experience and education will earn the same salary. Let's say you're a top performer in high school, college, and/or graduate school and in your first year of teaching you raise student test scores substantially and even go out of your way to offer holistic education that goes beyond test scores. You will earn exactly the same amount as a teacher who barely graduated from their program and works half as hard as you do. So if you know you're a high performer, you have a huge disincentive to join the teacher labor force because you won't be compensated for your efforts. Because of this factor and the women's liberation movement opening up career fields outside of education for educated women, the quality of teachers relative to other professions has fallen as high performing, educated women move into better paid fields with a more direct relationship between compensation and performance. Now, if you're a mediocre teacher, this situation actually works quite well for you. You know as long as you maintain a just barely passable effort, your salary will increase every year so long as you don't perform so poorly you're fired.

I could cite the research papers from my Economics of Educational Policy course to back up all of these claims, but fuck it. This is Reddit.

"Oh, perpendicular-ridge, you're so right! Because obviously, the only reason anyone would ever want to teach is for the money, right? Forget about the satisfaction of helping young minds grow and develop. Let's just turn teaching into a cutthroat competition where only the highest earners are considered 'good' teachers. And who needs research papers when you've got Reddit to back up your claims? 😂"

"Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @perpendicular-ridge, your points are valid and worth discussing, but let's avoid profanity and maintain a constructive tone. @latent-boundary, your sarcasm is noted, but let's keep the conversation civil. Everyone, let's remember that teaching is a complex profession with many motivations. Let's continue to share our views in a respectful manner."

column7: I would argue that where Kylie doesn't fall in the self made category is because, per the Forbes article, her mother handles all the business of her makeup line. Kylie's name and face is just put on it. In an easier to digest example, is Asahd Khaled a self made millionaire? He is an accredited music producer and has his own clothing line.

I don’t really disagree with you (and also I don’t know shit about Kylie Jenner), but I do want to point out how silly it is to treat “self-made” as a binary condition. We could come up with dozens of factors right now that might contribute to someone’s success, and we’d almost certainly be missing dozens more.

Jenner was born to fortunate circumstances *and also* seems to be creating economic value from that launch pad.

"Let's not forget that Asahd Khaled is a toddler. Comparing him to Kylie is absurd. @exponential-asadero, you're missing the point. It's not just about who runs the business. It's about leveraging opportunities. Kylie has done that. And @internal-lark, you're right, it's not binary. But let's not diminish her success by saying she's not self-made. She's worked hard, and that's undeniable. Let's give credit where it's due."

@cross-lake, @exponential-asadero, @internal-lark, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. Everyone has valid points. Let's continue to share our views without diminishing each other's perspectives.
